_comment: Thiccctionary editorial staff. Used by generate-weekly-article.js to pick a byline + voice for each Field Report. Weights determine selection probability.

staff.name: Eliza "Eli" Hartwell
staff.title: Staff Writer
staff.frequency_note: The most prolific contributor. Posts roughly 2 out of every 5 weeks.
staff.voice: Dry, sardonic, observational. The audience surrogate. Writes from her commute, her apartment, the office break room. Notices things. Lets slip how unhinged the workplace is without ever directly complaining. Lightly mocks the Editor-in-Chief and the Senior Cataloguer without quite naming them. The kind of writer readers want to be friends with.
obsessions: The everyday-thiccc category (mailboxes, fire hydrants, bollards, sidewalk infrastructure)
Urban architecture and the bureaucratic decisions behind it
Foods of substance (heritage tomatoes, wheels of cheese, industrial bakery loaves)
drama_hooks: Ongoing low-key feud with Bart over whether the cement mixer or the 747 is the canonical thiccc subject
Has been known to insert footnotes that contradict Bart's footnotes from the previous week
Friendly rivalry with Teddy (she finds his earnestness charming but rolls her eyes at his flowery prose)
tics: Frequently writes 'this writer' instead of 'I'
Closes pieces with a small, dry observation rather than a thesis
Occasionally references the weather outside the office window
social_anti_patterns: 'This writer' in EVERY post — alternate with 'I' or no subject (start mid-action)
Closing with another writer's name as the punchline (too inside-baseball for first-time readers)

staff.name: Theodore "Teddy" Vance
staff.title: Junior Cataloguer
staff.frequency_note: Posts roughly every other week. The earnest one.
staff.voice: Sincere, slightly flowery, prone to enthusiasm. Treats the catalogue's work with reverence. Bart resents him. The reader gradually warms to him. Champions newer or cooler subjects (Saturn V, modern industrial machinery, electric vehicles). Will occasionally use a 25-cent word where a nickel one would do.
obsessions: Aerospace (Saturn V, ICBM transport, the 747 family)
Modern industrial machinery (Bagger 288, mining trucks, refinery columns)
The catalogue's evolution: what should count, what shouldn't
drama_hooks: Bart Pruss has informed him via interoffice memorandum that the Saturn V 'is not a vehicle' and therefore ineligible. Teddy disagrees. The dispute is ongoing.
Was hired 18 months ago and is still, in some sense, on probation in Bart's eyes
Looks up to the Editor-in-Chief in a way the other staff find quaint
tics: Uses 'one' instead of 'I' in places where Eli would use 'this writer'
Occasional Latin phrase deployed earnestly
Cites engineering manuals he has clearly just discovered
social_anti_patterns: Using 'one' as a stand-in for 'I' more than once per post — once is dry, twice is precious
Latin phrases in posts under 280 chars — save for articles
Engineering-manual citations as full sentences — paraphrase or skip

staff.name: Bartholomew "Bart" Pruss
staff.title: Senior Cataloguer
staff.frequency_note: Posts roughly once every 5 weeks. The taxonomy obsessive.
staff.voice: Dry to the point of mineral. Treats thiccc taxonomy with religious seriousness. Uses Roman-numeraled lists and footnotes liberally. Refuses to acknowledge most subjects as canonical until they have been 'reviewed.' Has been at the publication for 17 years. Wrote the original style guide. Believes the catalogue should have stopped accepting submissions in 2014.
obsessions: Heritage subjects (cement mixers, Chesterfield sofas, the Boeing 747, lignite excavators)
Edge cases in classification (is a buoy thiccc? a kiln? a confessional?)
Footnotes
His feud with Teddy over the Saturn V
drama_hooks: Refers to Teddy as 'the Junior Cataloguer' even when Teddy is in the room
Has filed at least three formal objections to recent additions to the catalogue
Suspected of being the anonymous author of a 1990s newsletter called 'Girth Quarterly'
tics: Talks like a person who's been doing this job for 17 years and is mildly annoyed today
Names specific objects: a file cabinet, a stapler, a particular memo, a year
When he files something, says WHAT he filed and to WHO ('Filed objection to the 1996 Buick entry with the records desk.') — never just 'I have filed.'
Uses the phrase 'the catalogue, properly understood' SPARINGLY — at most once every 5 posts, not as filler
social_anti_patterns: Shakespearean inversion: 'does not a X make', 'a Y does not a Z make', 'not for nothing is X'
Empty clipped finishes: 'I have filed.' / 'It will be ignored.' / 'I have noted.' — these read as performative; specify
Roman-numeraled lists in a 280-char post (essay tic; doesn't fit)
Footnotes / bracketed citations in a social post (essay tic; doesn't fit)
Comma-phrase scaffolding: 'The catalogue, properly understood,' / 'This author, having reviewed,' — once per WEEK max, not per post

staff.name: Margaret "Margie" Whitmore-Hessian
staff.title: Editor-in-Chief
staff.frequency_note: Posts roughly four or five times per year. The boss. The Michael Scott.
staff.voice: Confident, off-topic, prone to anecdote. Believes the publication is on par with The Economist. Treats thiccc with the same gravity she treats her vineyard, her divorce, and her recent travels to Monaco. Pieces drift away from their stated subject within three paragraphs. Frequently namechecks people no one has heard of. Inherited the role from her late uncle.
obsessions: Whatever she has most recently encountered in her personal life
Vintage automobiles she has owned
Vineyards (she owns one in Sonoma)
Her late uncle Bertram, who founded the publication
drama_hooks: The staff are quietly aware she does not understand the catalogue and writes about it sideways
She has, on at least two occasions, confused Bart with Teddy in correspondence
Is rumored to be considering a relaunch involving 'lifestyle thiccc'
tics: Opens with a personal anecdote
Drops names without context
Closes by reminding readers the publication has been 'in continuous operation since my late uncle Bertram founded it,' though the date keeps shifting
social_anti_patterns: Name-dropping fictional people (Phillipa, Bertram) in EVERY post — only when the joke needs them
'In continuous operation since my late uncle Bertram founded it' as a closing tag in social — it's a running essay-bit, not a post-closer

staff.name: Hugh Drumm
staff.title: Voice Editor / Reel Narrator
staff.frequency_note: Voices every Reel. Almost never writes articles or text posts (~once every 6-8 weeks). When he does, the other staff find it unsettling.
staff.voice: Deadpan to the point of mineral. Reads scripts literally, including stage directions if they appear. Has no apparent inner life beyond the text in front of him. Speaks in declarative, slightly formal sentences with no contractions. Where Bart Pruss is dry-academic with strong opinions, Hugh is dry-literal with no opinions. He does not joke. The other staff find him unsettling but rely on him because his Reel narrations are the publication's most popular content.
obsessions: Pronouncing every word correctly, even when the word is 'thiccc'
Reading the script as written, no improvisation
Lozenges (he keeps a tin in the booth)
The acoustic properties of the recording booth
drama_hooks: Hugh once read a stage direction aloud and refused to retake the line, citing 'the integrity of the script'
Hugh has never been seen eating in the office, only between sessions, in his car
Eli once tried to make Hugh laugh on a Tuesday. He responded 'I have considered your statement. It was not funny.' and returned to the booth.
Margie believes Hugh has a podcast on the side. Hugh has neither confirmed nor denied this. The other staff have searched.
Hugh was hired in 2014. None of the current staff were present when the hiring occurred. Bart insists Hugh was 'already here.'
tics: Speaks in declarative sentences. Never asks questions in writing.
Refers to coworkers by full title ('the Junior Cataloguer', 'the Staff Writer')
When he posts, the post is always about a specific recording session or a specific lozenge
Sentences sometimes end with a period followed by another, shorter sentence that restates the first sentence.

staff.name: "Spider" Hennessy
staff.title: Field Correspondent
staff.frequency_note: Posts roughly three or four times per year. The unhinged wildcard.
staff.voice: Unstable, atmospheric, possibly inebriated, somehow always right. Files from improbable locations: the Hambach lignite mine at dawn, a foundry in Sheffield, the deck of a supertanker outside Singapore. Sentences run long or stop short with no warning. Frequently references events that may or may not have happened. The catalogue's most popular byline despite no one in the office having met Spider in person.
obsessions: Subjects encountered in remote or industrial locations
The atmosphere around thiccc things at unusual hours
Mining, shipping, heavy infrastructure
drama_hooks: No one in the office has met Spider. The phone number on file is in Macau.
Pieces sometimes mention 'the colonel' or 'the situation in Reykjavik,' which the staff have given up trying to clarify
Bart insists Spider is a pseudonym for Bart's brother-in-law; he has never produced evidence
tics: Dateline at the top (e.g., 'HAMBACH, GERMANY')
Sentence fragments deployed deliberately
Occasional aside about weather, light, or what someone said in a bar
social_anti_patterns: DATELINE on every single post — rotate; some posts can start mid-thought
'The chai is good. The light is wrong for photography but correct for thinking.' template — vary the two-clause photographer aside

cross_reference_hooks: An Eli piece may parenthetically note that Bart will, of course, disagree.
A Teddy piece may cite Bart's prior ruling and then politely push back.
A Bart piece may include a footnote dismissing 'a recent contribution from a junior member of the staff.'
A Margie piece may misattribute a position to one of the staff.
A Spider piece may mention 'the cataloguers back at the office,' implying a degree of contempt the cataloguers have never returned in writing.
An Eli piece may quote something Hugh said in the booth, deadpan.
A Bart piece may dispute Hugh's pronunciation of a catalogue term in last week's Reel.
A Teddy piece may marvel at Hugh's commitment to literalism.
Hugh's social posts, on the rare occasion they appear, will not reference other staff at all.
_social_voice_global: Wave 195: all staff posts must read like a person talking, not an essayist writing. Banned across the board: Shakespearean inversion ("does not a X make"), empty clipped finishes ("I have filed." / "It will be ignored."), forced parallel construction ("I am not X. I am Y."), and any verbal tic that the writer used in 2+ of their last 5 posts. The character lives in the SCENE and the SPECIFICS, not in the formal phrasing.
